title: Milestone reads can allocate an unbounded array from on-chain milestoneCount

evidence.quote:
const milestoneCount = (baseData?.[5].result as bigint | undefined) ?? 0n;
  const indices = Array.from({ length: Number(milestoneCount) }, (_, i) => BigInt(i));

reasoning: The hook trusts milestoneCount from the target contract and converts it to a JavaScript number before allocating an array and creating one read per milestone. If a bad address, malicious contract, or corrupted deployment returns a very large count, the browser can freeze or crash from the Array.from allocation and repeated contract read construction. The same unchecked conversion can also lose precision above Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER.
reproduction: Point useMilestoneEscrowRead at a contract whose milestoneCount returns a very large bigint such as 1000000000n. The render path attempts to allocate an array of that size before any pagination or cap can apply.
recommendation: Cap the maximum number of milestones fetched in this hook or add pagination. Avoid converting arbitrary bigint counts to number without bounds checks; return a capped count or an explicit overflow/error state.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No linked tests are provided, and the included files do not cover large or hostile milestoneCount values.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a hook test that mocks milestoneCount as a large bigint and asserts the hook does not allocate all indices, does not schedule an unbounded readContracts list, and exposes a bounded/error state.
minimumFixScope: frontend/src/lib/hooks/useMilestoneEscrow.ts plus a test for large milestoneCount handling.
